steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence opens with an appositive phrase: “A politician from South Carolina,” This phrase renames someone, so the noun it renames must immediately follow the comma as the subject of the main clause. That noun is Robert B. Elliott.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Elliott was elected to the US House of Representatives in 1870 by his constituents.: “Elliott” appears immediately after the comma as the subject — the appositive correctly renames him. ✓
  • Choice B — was the year that Elliott was elected to the US House of Representatives by his constituents.: No noun follows the comma — the clause begins with a verb, creating a fragment with no subject for the appositive to rename. ✗
  • Choice C — constituents of Elliott elected him to the US House of Representatives in 1870.: “Constituents” follows the comma, so the appositive would mislabel Elliott's constituents as the politician from South Carolina. ✗
  • Choice D — in 1870, his constituents elected Elliott to the US House of Representatives.: “In 1870” follows the comma — a prepositional phrase, not a noun, so there is nothing for the appositive to rename. ✗
That is the correct answer.
